title: Overfitting: When a Model Memorizes Instead of Learns
concept: Overfitting happens when a model memorizes the specific details of its training data too closely, instead of learning the general pattern — so it performs great on training data but poorly on new, unseen data.

exercises.prompt: A student memorizes the exact answers to 50 practice questions, word for word, but never actually understands the underlying method. On the real exam with different questions, what's most likely to happen?

options.text: They'll likely struggle, since memorized answers don't transfer to new, differently worded questions
They'll ace it, since memorization is the same as understanding
The exam will conveniently use the exact same 50 questions

exercises.explain: Right! Memorizing specific answers without understanding the pattern behind them rarely transfers to new questions.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model that scores perfectly on its training data is guaranteed to also perform well on brand-new data.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! A perfect training score can actually be a warning sign that the model just memorized those exact examples.

cards.title: Sam's practice quiz problem
cards.body: Sam memorizes the exact answers to every practice quiz question: "Question 7's answer is C." Sam aces every practice quiz. But on the real test, the questions are worded differently — and Sam, who never learned the actual concept, gets most of them wrong.

cards.title: What is overfitting?
cards.body: Overfitting is when a model memorizes the specific details of its training data too closely — including its quirks and noise — instead of learning the general pattern. It looks great on training data but performs poorly on new data.

cards.title: Memorizing vs. understanding
cards.body: An overfit model is like Sam: it memorized specific answers ("this exact photo is a dog") instead of learning the real pattern ("floppy ears and a wet nose usually mean dog"). Memorized answers don't help with anything new.

cards.title: An overfit dog-photo model
cards.body: A model trained on 100 dog photos might notice "every one of these dog photos has a green lawn in the background" and wrongly learn "green lawn = dog." It nails the training photos, but fails badly on a new dog photo taken indoors.

cards.title: A perfect training score isn't the goal
cards.body: It's tempting to think a model with 100% accuracy on training data is the best model. Often, it's the opposite — a suspiciously perfect training score is a classic sign of overfitting, not genuine learning.

cards.title: Learning the pattern vs. memorizing the data
cards.body: Two very different outcomes from training:

left.text:
High score on training AND
on new, unseen data

right.text:
High score on training,
low score on new data

exercises.prompt: A model scores 99% on its training data but only 52% on test data it's never seen. What does this strongly suggest?

options.text: The model has likely overfit — it memorized training details instead of learning a pattern that generalizes
The model is performing perfectly and is ready for real use
The test data must be broken, since training score was so high
The model needs more training data mixed directly into the test set

exercises.explain: Exactly! That huge score gap is the classic signature of overfitting.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Overfitting means a model has learned the general pattern extremely well.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Overfitting means the opposite — the model memorized specifics instead of learning the general pattern.

exercises.sentence: Overfitting happens when a model ___ the training data instead of learning the general pattern.
exercises.explain: Overfitting is exactly this: memorizing specific training examples rather than learning a pattern that generalizes.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes an overfit model?

options.text: A model that gets 100% on the photos it trained on but performs poorly on new photos
A model that performs consistently well on both training photos and brand-new photos
A model that hasn't been trained on any data yet

exercises.explain: Correct! A big gap between great training performance and poor performance on new data is the hallmark of overfitting.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model that performs similarly well on both training data and new, unseen test data has probably learned a genuine, general pattern.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Consistent performance across both sets is a good sign the model learned the real pattern instead of memorizing quirks.

exercises.prompt: A model trained to recognize handwritten digits scores 100% on its 5,000 training images, but only 61% on 1,000 new handwritten digit images. The team wants to explain this drop to their manager. What's the best explanation?

options.text: The model overfit — it memorized quirks of the specific training images instead of learning the general shape patterns of each digit
The new images must all be corrupted, since the model was perfect before
The model needs to be scored only on training data from now on
Handwritten digits are impossible for any model to learn

exercises.explain: Exactly! A steep drop from training to new data is the textbook sign of overfitting — memorization rather than general learning.

exercises.prompt: Comparing two students: Student A memorized answers to specific practice problems word-for-word. Student B worked through the same problems but focused on understanding the method behind each one. On a new exam with reworded questions, who is more likely to do better, and why does this mirror overfitting in models?

options.text: Student B, because understanding the general method transfers to new questions, while memorized specifics — like overfitting — don't
Student A, because memorizing more information is always better
They'll do equally well since both studied the same problems
Student A, because word-for-word memorization always beats understanding

exercises.explain: Right! Just like an overfit model, memorizing specifics without understanding the pattern fails to transfer to anything new.
